sentences.text: About two months probably, maybe two and a half.
I can't remember exactly the time.
Then the took us there in a truck.
And they took us there to Lublin, to the camp.
It was probably about 20 miles.
So before the-- we entered the camp, he said we're not clean, because in the camp, we have lice.
They has to give us a shower.
So he took us into-- it was a shower.
It was a bomb-- probably a warehouse, and he opened the water.
You know, city water, it has a big-- so with cold water, we start hollering, crying.
So he opened the door, he send in a German shepherd.
Then I got this.
[CRYING] And then they took us to the camp.
It was there about 4,000 Jewish people from the Polish war, prisoners of war.
So he took us to this camp.
There was already people, shoemakers, tailors, it was all shop there, all the people that work there.
So they gave me a new [NON-ENGLISH], a Polish [NON-ENGLISH], not the Russian.
Because they hate the Russian.
So then I can mix in with them.
If you go to work, then it's better for you.
The Polish people, if they catch a Jew, a civilian Jew, right away they had part to give him to the police.
Us, they wouldn't touch us, because we're Polish, from the Polish war.
It was easier to go out.
Because, this way, people had big stripes, and then recognize right away you are a Jew.
We wear the Polish [NON-ENGLISH].
Polish.
Not the striped one, no.
Because they took us to this camp.
And their camp was not stripes.
They all were Polish, Polish, Polish, from the war.
The war ended there two years.
They were all citizens there.
So they were working there.
So it was there.
Then he took us out the next day, took us out to work.
After they put you in that freezing shower, and then they gave you a uniform.
They didn't give me uniform there.
The people, the Jewish people gave me the uniform.
I went with the wet clothes there to the camp.
They gave you uniforms.
It would be too good for us.
Oh, yeah, assigned to the barracks, sure.
The barracks was-- the barracks-- three floors, bunk beds on the top there.
There was about 200 people, probably 300 people in each bunk.
It was barbed wire around the camp with Russian soldiers used to watch, or the Ukrainian people were the soldiers.
Not the Germans, they did it.
They watch us.
Yeah.
Then they gave me bread because they got already for the day so.
I was a new man, between Jewish people.
What's happened, happened, but, at least, now, with them together.
Yiddish and Polish.
Yeah.
They were mad on us.
They said they thought we will-- we will help them.
And then said you came to us, we thought the Russian will helping, not-- they said now you it was.
But they helped me.
They gave me clothes.
They gave me everything.
Oh, yeah, especially with the people we slept together.
Yeah, we were slept-- it was nothing.
It was just a piece of board.
Just your shirt in the back with-- that's it.
Jewish people.
I didn't met him before, but we got friends together.
I think one was-- I think one was-- I don't remember exactly.
Oh, yeah.
They took us out every morning, 4 o'clock in the morning.
Everybody goes out, and he count you.
And then he send you to work.
This guy goes here.
This guy goes there.
The first day, I went on the railroad to work on the railroad.
See, the partisans, every night, they used to blow out the railroad.
So we have to go there to work.
So the work on the railroad was in the cold weather, like in the winter.
You shouldn't wear gloves, shouldn't wear jacket, just with a shirt.
So if you picked the railroad, that's cold, frozen.
So the hand was sticking to the iron, but they wouldn't let you to put in nothing, just like this.
So I worked there.
And later, they transfer me in the cemetery.
They broke the stones in the cemetery, and they put on the driveways.
Because important, it was not all over the top, it was mud.
So they took this.
So this was the worst job in your life.
Because if a German stood in the back and he didn't hit you, you do slowly.
But when he stays in the back and hits you, so the stone falls on your feet and breaks you.
This was the worst, the worst, the worst thing to work.
But you have to do every day.
Every day.
So later, they transfer me to another job.
What other jobs did you do?
They went-- it was yeshiva there in Lublin.
So they went, they took us there to burn the yeshiva, we did this through the Torah, with everything, with everything.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And there we got a job.
For us, it was a good job.
We worked with SS police, 15 people.
But strong people because they have to give you back.
But they didn't bother you.
So if you did your job, it's OK.
So the guy-- the German guy who was watching us told them we were working so good, we need food.
Because we can't work without food.
So he said he'd give us a bread for five people more.
Then they took one guy from us and they gave a kitchen to make for us to eat.
And this kept us alive.
You see, because we were young.
If you have food, then you can work.
I was not afraid from work.
But if you're not have-- if you hungry, you can't.
So, of course, with working people did stripes, he hit-- the same German hit them and hit them.
And us, he didn't touch nothing.
So we used to have a good-- and a meal during the day.
So you were happy to work.
And they didn't bother us.
So you just do your job.
Like on the third floor, they have to take a box and turn, four people on the steps, but we did it.
We did it.
If you're not hungry, if you feed-- so you did it.
It was OK.
How were you chosen for this job?
There was Jewish police, was a Jewish fuhrer there.
He tell you, you go here, this goes here, this go here.
When, in fact, we went to work without a German even, just with the policeman of our camp, a Jewish police.
Because we know good we can't run away.
Where you run?
A German told us one time you can't run.
[GERMAN].
Where are you going?
So we did our job, and we came back.
Where you run?
You run away five steps, the Polish people will catch you right away.
You are Jew.
So we did the job and we were-- that's it.
It's far, they don't bother you.
So who picked you to do this?
The Jewish policeman there.
It was one German and one Jew.
He was a lieutenant in the Polish army, nice-- nice Fischer.
So he was the second then from him.
Like on Sunday, he took off the German SS, so he was-- he was in charge.
And he did a good job.
And there was two kapos, Jewish kapos in the camp.
They hit people more than the Germans there did.
Yeah, Kalicksman and Bailish.
This, I remember him good, these guys.
They did a job on my brother while there.
This is how I remember them good.
He hit him with a stick.
Blood was running from everything, because he said he didn't work too good on the job, because he wants to make sure he's good for the Germans, so he was alive.
So they kill them first later on.
They didn't waste time on him you know.
But before he did, he thought he will be a safe guy, he works for the Germans good.
It happens.
It was two guys like this.
Yeah, they did on him.
Yeah, but, later, I left the camp and I run away.
I left him there.
It was a year and a half almost.
Before-- before a couple weeks before I run away, later, I was waiting on to run way, so there was a big ghetto in Lublin, a big ghetto, thousands and thousands of Jews.
So they liquidate the ghetto, '43, I think it was.
So they took people from us, from our camp, to clean up the ghetto.
Then the Germans can move in, or Polish people can enter.
And the ghetto was everything.
Name it, it was in the ghetto.
They left.
Because they went in the night time, they took them away, and that's it.
So then I got friends with a Polish girl.
Near the ghetto, there was Polish people living.
So when we worked in the ghetto, like, for instance, I took a shirt to her.
And she gave me half a bread.
Next day, I took this, I gave her this.
This girl was [INAUDIBLE], she was there.
And that way, we were talking, talking.
Probably she fall in love with me, I don't know.
And she said, if you want to run away, I take your hand.
We'll run away.
Where you going to run away?
I don't know.
Do I know where she is?
And then we didn't have anybody with us.
You have to feed people.
People are living on a farm, a poor farm is-- so I told her OK.
But how can I go run away without having nothing.
So I told her, you know what, let your brother come here with a wagon.
We packing these old thing, we packing in boxes for the Germans.
The Germans takes away.
So I'll start there.
I'll put on this wagon.
Your brother can have it there later.
You have money to feed us.
Because, four people, you have to have even once a day, you need money.
They were poor people.
If they didn't have enough bread, they have to go out to buy.
So the neighbors would look around.
How come she buys more bread, this and this?
She have to be very careful because this was-- we were right, it was a house and a house.
So she said OK.
So I'll pay a little bit, this and this.
And then, to run away, we needed two Polish people to take us there.
So in Poland, it was a horse and a wagon.
I think they got in New York, you know the two-- the two wheels with a horse and wagon?
So we need two guys.
And they want money.
And we don't have money.
They want $500, $1,000.
We don't have any money.
So we got work there in the ghetto.
I saw big fur coats, man's fur coat.
There was plenty there.
You can take gold everything.
There was on the floor, they left it.
So I took the two coats and gave her.
She can give them, then will take us.
And they did it.
She did everything.
You name it, she did it.
Everything what I say, she did it.
She was gold.
Then you have to leave the camp.
If you run away from the place which you work your people, they kill 10 people.
On this side, they kill 10 people, this side.
So I wouldn't do this.
When I run away from the work, they kill the people for nothing.
So I have to go out.
So I went to the Jewish guy, he was the second tent from the German.
And I told him I'd like to run away.
He was good, you can trust him.
Because a lot of Jewish fuhrers, they were kapos.
You couldn't say it.
He said, OK, you run away.
Where you go run away?
So I told him I'm from the Russian army, and I know a lot of Russians in the woods there.
And I will go with them.
He said, and if they catch you?
I told him if they catch me, I wouldn't say you told me because they will kill me and kill you and her.
So he said, OK, let's see.
A couple days later, I said I like to go to the ghetto to work there.
And he told the guy, the German SS, [GERMAN].
He told them we're going to the hospital, four people to the hospital.
And I hear what he's saying then.
Honest to God, you have to have blood in this.
And we're shaking.
And he heard he told him the four people, the guy-- [GERMAN].
And we went away.
Because I know them.
The all the time we were together.
So I told him, I wouldn't go alone.
I'd like to have the three people with me because-- I wouldn't go.
And I know she will say yes.
I know she will say yes.
Yeah, it was-- one was Shlomo.
One was Bill.
One was Ziskind.
So then we were prepared already.
We got in-- like in the army, they give you a sack with you.
So I put shirts there.
I put everything.
And we went out to the camp.
And we went through the house with a Polish woman there, her aunt.
We were sitting there till 4 o'clock. 4 o'clock, they came, the two Polish people with us.
Two people were in one wagon.
Two people was another wagon.
She was sitting in the one and another Polish girl was sitting in another one.
So we went through the whole city like we go to our wedding, like in Poland, we got to our wedding.
We went there.
So about two miles, we stop because I didn't want to know that the Polish people there where we are.
So we told them you stop here.
We'll go there.
There was the first-- the first night before Christmas, the first night before Christmas.
This in '43, 1943, and Christmas time.
So we went there.
She opened the door from the bottom and boom.
And that's it.
I don't know where I'm going, where she is.
I don't know her father.
I didn't nobody.
What could we-- we took our chance.
And it was nothing.
And she kept us, all four till the war ended.
Well, first of all, we digged a whole there in the barn where the cows and the pigs stay.
Dig the hole.
And from the wall, we made an entrance there to go there.
So what was a problem, it's nowhere to put the dirt what we digged, because the neighbors will see.
Something goes on, how come it's dirt under the sill.
So in the night time, the brother used to take the horse with a wagon, and took the dirt and spread on the fields there.
So we had a place to run away or to hide there.
But we were sitting there not every day.
We were on the barn upstairs.
We were sitting there.
And she used to bring us, in the morning, something to eat, and in the afternoon, something to eat.
That's it.
In the night time, she used to take us to the house to sit there a couple of hours with her, to-- but in the night time, you saw, we didn't talk there.
You couldn't talk because the Polish people who are neighbors, they can hear every word.
Because I heard them talking, It's good when the German kills the Jews.
I heard them talking.
So we didn't talk.
So in the night time, I like to see if I have my voice.
So you say-- [COUGHING], just to make sure I got still something in my voice.
So that's it.
They were good, very good people.
So we had a problem one time.
They had a boy.
He was mental.
So we were watching not to see us.
One day, he came straight to the barn, he knocked on the door there.
And I thought that this is the-- she bring us food or water.
So I opened this, and I see he's there.
So what to do?
So I have to wait until she comes to bring us the food.
So I told her, his name was Zich.
So Zich saw us.
And she said, oh, what are you going to do now?
What are you going to do now?
So I told her, we'll go away.
She said, no, don't go away.
If you go away, they'll catch you right away here and they will kill.
She took us to her sister in the village in the night time.
So they were so smart.
The sister ask her, how come you don't take them?
She said because Zich is crazy, so I'm afraid for them.
We were already there half a year.
They took us there.
We were there five, six days.
And the guy came to us.
He said I can't keep you.
The little boys, they run after me and they will catch you.
So I said, OK, tell her back and let her take us back.
She came in the night time.
She took us back there.
And we were there til the end of the war.
We were in the night time walking.
It was a village near our village, not too far.
Not too far.
Sure, but you're not-- the Germans, if you don't say nothing you're a Jew, they wouldn't bother you.
The Polish people, the Ukrainian people, for them, you have to watch.
But they recognize you.
If you talk, oh, you talk like a Jew.
You look like a Jew.
The Germans, they don't know nothing.
They don't know nothing.
You can go with them if you wouldn't say nothing.
They don't know you're a Jew.
It's not written you are Jew.
But the Polish people, they look like this, oh, he talks like a Jew.
He talks like a Jew.
So one time, during the there, I was crazy.
I said if we go to Lublin, maybe I can save my brother in law.
I took a chance.
So I go there.
And so there was, in the village, was a guy with two horses with a wagon, and he took passengers to the city.
I went there too.
And I have to pay 10 zloty, the money, and I went inside.
After 5, 10 miles, he stop the horses to take a-- to take a rest.
So everybody goes out.
So I'm sitting there and looking.
Do I have to go out or to sit?
So if I would sit there, maybe people think how come he doesn't go out.
So I decide I will go out too.
I went out.
And then, later, we have to go back.
So he checks everybody's tickets, sees the ticket.
So I show him the ticket.
I show him the ticket, I see right away something fishy.
He looks too much to me.
He said, from where are you going?
I wouldn't tell him from where I'm going.
So I would-- she was always talking another village, so I told him from another village.
And then he asked me, do you know somebody there?
And my blood is already cooking.
I told him, [NON-ENGLISH] my name.
Then he tells me do you have the document, to show him a document.
So I have a document.
I took her brother's document, without a picture, his name, [NON-ENGLISH].
So he ask me, how come you don't have a picture?
But I'm smart enough to answer him.
So I told him, I lost my passport.
So in the meantime, they give me this piece of paper without a picture.
Well, it was kosher.
Then he said, you know, I have nothing to do with you.
I know you, you're not kosher.
You're a Jew.
But I wouldn't say nothing, but I can't take you with me there.
He was right.
Because there, before you go into the city, the police, they're there.
[INAUDIBLE], being a black market, said they will kill me.
They'll kill you too.
I can't take you.
So he left me in the middle of the road like this.
Left in the middle out like this.
So I decided, no, I wouldn't go back to my place.
I will go to Lublin.
I will go to Lublin.
And I went there to Lublin.
And after a couple of miles, I see police, Polish police and German police, going on bicycles.
So I thought maybe they told him already there.
But I went there through.
So I went to look for my brother.
And I know where he works later.
I went there to look for my brother in law.
So the little boy start following me, following me.
And that's it.
I don't have nothing to do.
So I started to run away in the fields.
The Ukrainian people got after me.
And I heard, in Russian, he thought to him, don't shoot.
So I stopped, and they took me back to the same camp.
To which camp?
In Lublin, the same camp when I run away, they took me back.
And they hit me.
They make me black and blue.
So after probably about two weeks, I decided I have to run away.
So the camp was all around it wires.
After the camp was a cemetery, a cemetery.

And the Ukrainian people, the Ukrainian guy was watching, was back and forth, back and forth. 4 o'clock in the morning, when they took us out for the count us over there, I sneak out from the roof to there, and I went out the cemetery.
It was dark a little bit.
I went out the cemetery.
And I have to go out.
In Poland, the cemeteries have big gates, iron gates.
And a watch man stays there and watch.
I see the guy's there.
I said, now, I'm lost.
Because if they catch me again there, they will cut pieces of me.
So I decided I will fool him.
I took my hand like this, and he thought it's a gun.
A gun.
And he didn't say nothing.
He opened the door, and I'm walking like this.
And I keep still like this.
I keep still like this.
And I went there, and I went back to the same place where we're hiding out.
And when she saw me, she said, oh.
[POLISH], you're still alive.
Was nearly before '44, before the liberation.
It was a half a year before the liberation, but--
